text: like all large street markets you have to wade through tons of crap to find the jewels but they are there. Some decent clothing and antique fixtures amid a sea of Chinese made junk

I am often in Rome and it has been 10 years since I went to the market as it had become just a chinoiserie bazaar. Instead I found hundreds of stalls selling clothing, objects, books as old as it was 30 years ago. That market is coming back. Let's hope...

Walked round the whole market from clothes bags and shoes section which wasn’t that interesting The main area where you can find interesting items is in the middle section where some stalls sells old coins and currency as well as watches and clocks Spend a good two hours and to properly go round you will be walking close to 7km plus Happy hunting !

I make a point to go here whenever I can-- been about 10 times in the last few years.  There are a lot of things here however I suggest you get there early in the morning as the crowds by late morning/ early afternoon are hard to negotiate.  There are several areas to find antiques and depending on your entrance to the market(I used to approach it from the main entrance however prefer to go right to the antique area off of Circonvallazione Gianicolense)--it is a little further to walk (I stay in Trastevere) however I find it easier to negotiate from this entrance.  The vendors are a lot of fun (just be nice) and there are some real treasures to find just take your time-- it is sensory overload.

It is only on Sundays, you can walk here or take a bus, to Porta Portese, from here the Flea Market starts from there, lots of clothing, it used to be different, but still a few items worth buying....your choice if you have nothing else to do.

Located in the Trastevere district, this is a huge flea market where you can find everything from antiques to knick-knacks, from gastronomy to clothing both new and used. With the right attention and a little luck you can find good deals.

Came here for the benefit of my 3 teenagers and ended up buying 4 super lightweight summer shirts for €10 - bargain! The girls bought stuff too. As others say, it is very very big - the stalls all stretch out along one very long road. They’re all rather similar, selling mainly cheap clothing, not much else in terms of bric a beach and certainly no antique type stuff that you get in other flea markets. Some food stalls along the way and further in some cafes kind of ‘behind’ the main market. Although shaded, it was incredibly hot when we went - hot even for Rome in August - so take some water. We were careful about thieves having been targeted unsuccessfully at the Forum, but I didn’t notice anything obvious. Worth a visit if you just want some cheap clothes bargains but not if you’re expecting to be entertained by curios or to find something you didn’t expect. And great for men’s summer shirts, in all sizes.

This was a great flea market, but don’t go expecting anything but a flea market.  There are booths with cheap Chinese stuff, but also vintage clothing, antiques, Peruvian clothing, Indian jewelry, shoes, purses, souvenirs, and lots of fur coats.  It seems that the best area for antiques was well into the center which took us a good hour to get to, albeit we were shopping. (did I mention HUGE) We gave up after seeing the market extended past the major cross street.  Even turning back we logged a good two miles on the Fitbit.We came prepared for pickpockets, stowing anything valuable safely away, and keeping bags in front and had no problems.

This was like Petticoat Lane meets Brick lane with sections of new items , antique stalls and junk stalls mixed together.Mostly new clothes / shoes with some second hand clothes and house hold items. There is a small quantity of bric a brac / antique stalls. Prices vary hugely between stalls and you need to haggle. Very interesting to watch the locals and see what is for sale. Look out for pickpockets, especially when crowded.

While I don't like these flea markets this one is a bit better than others my wife has made me go along with her to. Generally, I would prefer to be waterboarded than to go to these places.  However, on the sunday that we went in May it was not so crowded as most markets and the quality of the goods seem better. It is mostly clothes, leather goods, and shoes. These seem to be very good and my wife made some good buys. You will get tired of hearing the hawkers yell Prego! Prego@ Prego!.   I wish I had known in Italian how to say Shut Up.  Too much.  If you can stand a long walk this market would be good exercise.

We caught s taxi from the Trevino fountain area - €9. Great for a mouth around - lots of fake goods , clothes and bricks brick a brak area. We then walked down the riverbank to St. Peter's square for the 12o'clock blessing - took about 45 minutes to walk .

The Sunday market consists of mostly Chinese knock offs and 1 Euro stalls and was not really my cup of tea. However, the bicycle stalls on the "first floor" were really interesting and had some real gems in there (although most of the bikes were in line with the cheap and nasty theme of the Sunday market). Cool spot if you are into vintage bikes!

Yes, it has lost its peculiarity, but it's still fun walking around the market.You can find a bit of everything, if you come here make sure you buy some stuff, otherwise you'll remember only the crowd.

We went to the Porta Portese Market on our first day in Rome. It is a big market, easy to find. Here you can buy everything from toothpaste and sunglasses to antique furniture. Some stalls are cheap, others are more expensive. My advice is to check prices on different market stalls before you buy

My wife and I went looking for vintage goods.  We found that vintage items were mostly clustered at the far end of the market (and off of the main aisle away from the archway entrance and further from the bridge).  Once there, we found some great records and a few fun vintage items. Pricing by the vendors is aggressive, so be prepared to walk away.
Try to visit it early in the morning, before hundreds of visitors come. You can really find anything here, from cheap Chinese fake goods, to antique furniture.

As flea markets go, there isn't a lot of older or antique stuff.  Instead, it is a good place for cheap souvenirs, shoes, clothes (lots of clothes), luggage, and household goods.  It is fun to people watch and haggle - but make sure to haggle!  Don't take the first price, ever!  If you recognize it for what it is, it is a fun Sunday morning. There was a police presence and we did not feel unsafe, as many of the other reviews talked about pickpockets.  Just don't be dumb!

something for everyone here. There's lots of car boot style stuff and junk and general market things like fruit and veg and cheap clothes but there are also expensive antiques, antique Italian  furniture, great bargains and gems to find and lots of retro as well as new stuff too so it is worth a look around. It is an interior designer's dream ace to pick up a bargain, but you must haggle. Also as a tip, decide the max amount you will pay for something and stick to it and get the money out and ready to count it out in from of the traders as they are more likely to sell things at a bargain when they actually see the cash in from of them. The market is usually open from dawn to 4pm ish every Sunday and is in the Trastevere area, over the bridge the Vatican side of the water, just south of St. Peter's  on the map. About 10 to 15 mins walk from St. Peter's. Keep your money, bags and valuables close to you though as it's renowned for pick pockets. Also take some bags with you in case you buy anything as often the sellers don't have carriers to put your purchases in.

I love a market and have seen quite a few in my time.. If you really expect to go to any market nowadays and uncover genuine bargains, antiques and treasures worth passing on to your children, you will be sadly disappointed.  I often think I can imagine people working till the early hours of the morning before the market, attaching dust and grime on to the "antiques", or scratching them to make them look genuine.On a Sunday morning, you can take a long walk to Trastevere, over the Tiber River, or take a number 8 tram to reach this market.  I thought it was much better than Campo de Fiori, also in Rome, El Encants in Barcelona or El Rastro in Madrid and spent a couple of happy hours here.You can buy new and used clothing, bags and leather goods, costume jewellery, piles of junk pretending to be antiques and curios, shoes, shoes and shoes, furniture, computer and smartphone accessories.  The market is vast and there is a big crush of people.  Heed the usual warnings about pickpockets.In general, the traders are good natured and some are roguish.  One of them spoke to me in French and offered to reduce a pair of pants labelled at 48 euros to 40 euros, "because you are French", he said.  I had already seen very similar pants on sale in downtown Rome for 25 euros and anyway, I'm not French.  But he was so convincing, that it almost bought them.  I couldn't be sure if any of the smartphone products, such as portable chargers, were duds or not, so was wary of buying any.  Afterwards, I strolled the long walk in to the city, stopping for a lunch of grilled calamari at one of the many food outlets in this district.I thought it was a morning well spent.

I've read a few negative reviews on here - not sure what you're expecting from a market if you hated this place. It is exactly what a market should be.Yes, there's cheap stuff.Yes, there's fake stuff.Yes, vendors will always try to get a few extra dollars out of you. They're trying to make money!As in all marketplaces, be prepared to walk away and the price will come down. I had fun, found exactly what I wanted - pair of shorts, some scarves, a hat and a shirt for my wife, and a cap for me. All up, three hours there cost approx. €15 and we left happy with our purchases.It's a market - a big market, not a boutique mall. Be prepared.

At Porta Portese you can buy almost everything, from Made in China-style cheap stuff, scarfs, shoes, clothes (used from 50 cents and up, and new), vintage items, bags, makeup, fake and real jewelry and much more. A lot of stands have the same stuff, and the market is BIG!Make sure to bargain (you don't want to find the same item at a lower price later), hold on to your valuables, don't bring money/phone ecc if you really don't need them, do bring coins... Here, cash is king. Don't forget that you can take the train to the Trastevere station and then walk 5 minutes to the market. Most of the old, cool, vintage stuff are at the via Ippolito Nievo/Largo Francesco Anziani/via Ergisto Bezzi intersection. If you want vintage or used stuff, come early. Like 6-7 AM.Tip: Bring a big shopping bag because the ones they give you rip easily. And you'll end up carrying 5 ripped plastic bags.

4 of our 6 traveling to Rome spent about 2 hours traipsing through the hundreds of vendors.Very much like most American flea markets this one featured inexpensive clothes, both new and used, cheap electronics and a lot of inexpensive souvenirs.  There ARE a few dealers selling higher end items, especially books.  They are few and far between.  Most interesting was the 3 card monte con game being run in plain view of the several police patrolling the flea market.The market is probably close to a mile long so be prepared for a long hike if you want to explore everything

We got tipped on this market by a local guide. You can probably buy almost everything here and most prices can be bargained down (to our experience). Dont expect too much from the goods sold here, but take it as an experience. Due to the sheer size of the market, you will probably not have time to see it all.

We came early. About 8:30 AM. It was perfect timing. The sun wasn't that bad until 10 o'clock. It wasn't crowded yet, so we could walk freely. Porta Portese is huge. Sells all kinds of things: new and used; modern electronics and antique stuff; stolen phones and original brand perfumes; souvenirs and home essentials. If you forgot your charger at home or the SD card in your camera is full, 5 euro and you're fine.My advice: don't buy that new and cheap Chinese stuff (clothes, shoes, underwear). They have a lot of it around the city and it's all the same. You can find something used instead and it's gonna be unique. Try looking for a nice fabric like silk or linen.

I overheard a person comment "where do they get all these worthless items". Yes, there are lots of worthless items buy lots of good items as well. You really have to take your time searching. If purchasing clothing.. Most of the clothing tables are a mess. I searched a few for a couple of minutes each and always found something nice in different sizes. I did not purchase much but I managed to get a very nice vintage purse for 10 euros. I walked away from it the first time because the price was not right. I purchased it at the lower price almost at closing time and on my way out. Don't hesitate to make an offer. The flea markets where I live in NJ are the same so I was not expecting to find much more than I did even in such a huge place. Flea markets are just that and not high end superstores.

This is not really a flea market, but a cheap jewelry, cheap accessories, clothing items and souvenirs market.There are a few shops that sell lamps and other flea market stuff but they are few and some are not on the main street.This place is huge and it takes a good 2 hours to walk all the way to the end of the stands. We had the time and the girls wanted to get some souvenirs for their friends. All in all we had a nice time just walking around, haggling and buying some junk. Go if you like this type of pass-time; Don't bother if you are on a tight schedule or if you are looking for antiques.

Take bus #75 from termini station and it drops you off directly in front of the gate. Be prepared for crowds. Mostly clothes, handbags, costume jewellery and the odd bits and pieces. Goes on and on for what seems like miles and miles. Arrived at 12.00 and left when closing at 14.30. Only need one visit. Lots of local Italians were shopping however strangely 80% of the vendors were not local at all. Came away with a few bits and pieces for the children. If you go remember to bargain and walk away if the price is not to your liking - not worth the aggravation. Was fun but very very busy. Note - we did not see any toilet facilities.

This market is nice; if you are looking for cheap clothes, shoes, bags, (ehh well...everything) you will find it. Can't say anything about quality though. It is nice to walk around, have a look, have a talk, watch your pockets....and wander away when you're tired of it.

so crowded, but we've bought souvenirs for 1 euro, which was sold in other places at 3 euro. Also there is a range of vintage bags from 3 euro till 50 (for the designers bags, as celine, & burberry), the books where nice, just ask for Marco Polo who sells great arts books for just 2 euro the book.
huge market with all kinds of stuff, quite cheap, very busy and beware of pickpockets. ALways good to buy some presents.

I've walked the hopeless, energetic, frustruating, suffocating and fun crows of Porta Portese four times. I might not have returned quite so many times had I not had new friends or relatives with me each time. As there were first-timers on each visit, we just had to go to Porta Portese. Remember that it is only open on Sunday mornings!This humongous flea market is comprised by a couple of main streets and an assortment of small side-streets stock full of sellers. Before going there, it would actually be a good idea to purchase a good enough map of Romeand to memorize the layout of the place. The only decent one I know is the 1:12500 map by Touring Editore – a priceless companion to any traveler in the city, and it's dead cheap! (My original copy is from 2003, a battered loyal guide that gave way to the new expanded edition that I bought in September 2012.)  This will make it much easier to walk around and not miss the most interesting stuff on the small streets. The main avenues are full of cheap textiles, gizmos familiar from shopping channel, stolen hygienic products and cigarettes resold as supposedly legit et cetera. The antiques and miscellanea are elsewhere.I'm actually grateful to a friend who had prepared thus and showed me a few streets I hadn't seen before, even though I had been there three times! There's no decent food sold at the market as far as I know, which is a shame. Decent falafel, but one doesn't come to Rome for falafel.

Opens at about 9am, shuts down at around 2pm. I caught the number 3 bus (stops Viale Trastevere) from/to Manzoni. The bus also goes past the Colosseum.I had a lot of fun at the market. But then I also really like open air markets. Got a leather belt, hat and sunnies for â¬15 all up. Snacked on a panini for â¬3. It's nothing special considering everything there is to do in Rome, but I had a free Sunday morning and it was worth it for me to check out.You have to haggle with the shop keepers, and that was a lot of fun. English is doable, but I happen to know Bengali (most of the people working there are from Bangladesh) so that made it easier since I can't speak Italian.I say check it out if you've got the time and if you like buying things cheap.

It took a little finding, but if you take the No 8 tram from Largo Argentina ask someone when you are say 4 stops past the Tiber: "Porta Portese??" [POR TAR POR TAY SAY?]. There is a stop right at one edge of the market. Those reviewers disappointed by the market may have taken the wrong street, there are a couple of less crowded less interesting areas.AND GO ON A SUNDAY! Not open other days.There are, in addition to such new items as top Levis with labels upside down or wrong size, for next to nothing, new leather bags, and a wondrous array of used garments (great light-weight gifts to take home) there are some serious old or antique items including books, prints, maps, glassware, etc. As the items get more 'antique' and as the end of the market morning, haggle hard (preferably numbers in Italian) expect vociferous and lightning fast exchanges, and if you reach a conclusion on price, do be a good person and buy the thing. Nothing ruder than chiseling tourists who walk away after the bargaining, spoils the process for others.The market is very crowded, wheelchairs only for the brave with company!There is food available from stalls in the market. Or cross the viale Trastevere (tram route) and go a little further uphill to find interesting eating places.OR, exiting the market at Porta Portese itself, upstream end, by the river, cross the bridge and ask your way to the Testaccio market, excellent food market off the tourist routes. And from Testaccio an excellent walk back to the centre via the Circus Maximus, etc.

Every Sunday from 7am to 2pm, hundreds and hundreds of stalls, selling everything from dolls to clothes to antiques to coins to fake designer purses to jewelry to electronics and everything in between.  Bartering is a given, get ready to sift through a bunch of crap to take home a couple awesome treasures.

Visit itself rather short, less than an hour is enough; structure still in perfect condition and wonderful from an architectural point of view.You waste time especially waiting in line to buy a ticket for € 5 at the totems near the entrance: a lot of crowds, to which is added the one always present in the square.

This was an interesting tour narrated by Rick Steves, which could have been downloaded regardless of paying for the tour. However, given the ticket line, it was good to have purchased the tickets in advance so we didn’t have to wait.

We arrived at 7:30am and there was no one around, great for external pics. Had to wait for opening. Do not eat or drink in the places in the piazza  we did food was horrible and expensive  bit needed the toilet.Going inside judt crazy, loads of people, but enjoyed the audio guide - an incredible buiding.
The Pantheon fantastic of course, but the time to observe it guided is very little and inside the guide can not speak, better to do it alone.

A small building, only one room but there are quite a few things to see in there. We had an audio guide which definitely helped. The gift shop had run out of guide books which was frustrating as we would have liked to learn more about the history of the building. We bought skip the line tickets which was a good idea as the queue to buy tickets was very long.
We walked around Rome and passed the Pantheon, but there was such a queue to the entrance so we skipped going in. We walked around and looked from outside.
entry had 5.- pp. Very nice but we're going around fast. Pretty from the outside, too. There can be people so plan time.
It's a pity that the Pantheon costs admission in the meantime. It would be nice if you could continue to see this magnificent structure for free.
Very crowded place as the square on which it is located is quite small. It is worth visiting only if you have never seen it and if you have several days available.

Not my first visit to the Pantheon but always worth seeing. Queue to enter was quite long, perhaps 30 minutes, and this is a function of process. Tickets have to be bought from a touch screen and everybody has to enter their name and e-mail which is tortuous. Last time I went you gave them €5 cash and walked in. This rush to digitisation has to stop. It is not helpful. The site itself is fascinating although, personally, I would rather see it's true Roman historic side rather than it.misappropriation by the Catholic church. It is, however, a must see attraction

Via Tiqets.com we had ordered tickets in advance: fast line and audio tour. When we got there, no one from this organization was to be found. Despite emailing, texting and calling....no contact. But what I'd like to leave you with: stand in line on the left. Pay cash €5 and you're in in no time. Our experience was that this queue also went faster than the queue of all people with pre-purchased tickets. I just don't know if you can still buy an audio tour inside if you're interested. It is worth taking a look inside at the design, paintings and sculptures. Our money, incidentally, has now been refunded. Without apology. It's too bad.Later our money without explanation

Loads of people in the mid morning , and it was closed to the public until 12:30 because of a mass. I have been inside before and it blew me away before. Go c early to avoid crowds and if you can, hope for a sunny day so that you catch the ray of light inside

Tip # 1-Forget buying tickets in advance. I tried to get advance tickets online from Canada from the official Pantheon website which was a total disaster. After 4 unsuccessful tries, I gave up.  Tip # 2-Avoid the first Sunday of the month (since most museums are free for Italians and the lines can be very long), instead we showed up on a week day in November and waited 10 minutes in the cash line ( 5 Euros per person) and got in. All the other people who bought tickets in advance waited in longer lines. Basically, don't bother with advance tickets. Once inside you can stay for as long as you like. The Pantheon (an architectural wonder) is totally worth seeing. Tip # 3-Watch Manuel Bravo ( an architect) on You Tube for his in depth explanation of the Pantheon. His videos about various ancient buildings in Italy were very educational and probably provided more information than some guides.

The queue to get in is huge but it moves at a good pace. While the place is impressive to see there are not many places to stop at, it is just a circular room with statues and carvings in the wall. The guided tour points out the history about several things which is interesting to learn.From start to finish it wont take long.
In addition to being a historical work, where there are levtombs of the Fathers of the Fatherland, the Pantheon is also a great architectural work, with its large dome. Endless line, unfortunately

We arrived at another tour book attraction. This is the famous Pantheon. However, we are just the appearance, and everyone is knocking in front of the door even "to this tour". The All-Temple, an ancient Roman temple dedicated to the gods in Roman mythology, later turned into a Catholic church and renamed the Church of the Virgin Mary and Martyrs (Santa Maria ad Martyres). Roman mythology and Greek mythology have a lot in common.The Holy Temple belongs to the "B-class Temple" in Rome.

It's a mess about the queues. If you go without entry and you have to pay there is the queue on the right most where the screens are. Then they make you stand in another line to get in. Taking this off, once you get in, you don't want to get out. It is brutal to see everything inside and you are left thinking how far they were able to go to build such a monument

Purchased tickets online , we had to queue to pick up the “real” tickets at an office about 10 minutes away. Beware that there is a line so go in advance. Although the reservation stated that the audio guides were delivered inside the Pantheon, they were delivered to us in this office and at the end of use must be returned to the same place.Another thing to report is that the skip-the-line priority ticket is not really skip-the-line.... you wait in line for everyone who like you has the same reservation. Actually the people who wait impresses but with a little patience you scroll pretty fast (we waited about 45 minutes on November 01 but believe us, we thought we would stay there at least a couple of hours). It is also possible to enter with a ticket purchased locally. The church is very beautiful and characteristic and is used for functions so you have to respect silence and order. The audio guide helped to understand the story and the content. Duration: 45 min - 1 hour. Recommended

The building is impressive and if you know the history of how it has been built, even more. However, despite its spectacularness, I’m not sure it’s worth 1 hour of queuing and paying admission, even if it’s only 5 euros. In Rome there is much, but much to see

of all the Roman monuments, the one I wouldn't forget and this one. First, it should be noted that queues could discourage more than one. The staff is helpful but uncompromising. You might think you're smart enough to take your ticket with the automatic machines availableq; alas, once you have the ticket in your hands, you have to join the queue of those who have a ticket. I therefore recommend choosing a line that is long, of course, but that goes through a counter run by one person. Payment (€5) by credit card only.The interior is impressive and it is worth taking your time to read the various infoamtions. Tomb of the painter Raphael and two Italian sovereigns.
There are too many tourists to watch slowly. I only saw the exterior. I think it's better to be very careful about pickpocketing because there are too many people.

Very impressive building and well worth the trip. To get in was very confusing though. To the left side of the entrance are 2 lines. You need to stand in the outermost line to buy your ticket from a machine. Then you move to the innermost line to get in. There are very few signs and tourists were asking each other what they were supposed to do.
A 2,000 year old Roman temple that became Catholic around 600 AD. So the top while unique with the rotunda or dome, the rest looks like any grand church in Europe
This was our second time here and I would say that we spent most an hour around might even be a stretch. Think you can get through this in 30 to 45 minutes.

I pre booked tickets via a website called Oh my guide! Which seemed to be the official website and as pre booking was recommended I thought it was a good idea, until we arrived; our tickets appeared to be for a guided tour and cist 15 euros each, entrance is only 5 euros so ripped off straight away.  The Pantheon staff out front who were checking tickets were so rude, saying these tickets were fake, not valid for entry & needed to be collected at the Oh my guide shop, which they claimed to not know where it was even though they said it was 2 minutes walk away!!! We couldn't find the office so as we had other things booked we went back the next day, with help from a free map we picked up along the way we eventually found the office who admitted they are a separate company and that Pantheon staff don't like people using them, and that all our ticket was for was for entry and that it's not a guided tour. I managed to get our entry tickets out if them but the shop actually took us about 3 hours to find which was a waste of time and money and took the enjoyment out of visiting a beautiful building
Stay away from the ice cream shop in the corner whşch charge 15 eur for one icecream , come on! İn trevi and evrrywhere it is 5eur only and much better taste
It’s a beautiful and well-preserved building and also very crowded. Perfect timing to visit is crucial. Otherwise immerse yourself in the wave of people inside the Pantheon.

The Pantheon in Rome was built by Emperor Hadrian around 2,000 years ago, around 126 AD. Before it was converted into a Christian church, it served as a temple dedicated to all the pagan gods of ancient Rome. Today, the Pantheon also houses the tombs of several iconic historical figures. Later, it was consecrated as a Christian basilica and dedicated to the Virgin Mary.Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio) (d. 1520) – The world-renowned Renaissance painter and architect. His tomb is marked with the inscription:Here lies Raphael, by whom Nature feared to be outdone while he lived, and when he died, feared she too would die.We had the fast track ticket which we picked up the day before and our time window was between 12- 1pm. We reached there by 1 pm and looked over the temple within 50 minutes. There are no WC/ toilets near the area expect from the few resturants  that provide it for their guests but many of them were unclean.

Don't both with the skip-the-line tickets. If you arrive around 9.30am or 10am, you can walk right in and buy a ticket. In fact, the queue for online tickets with audio guides, which you have to collect at an office about ten minutes away, took longer to move than those turning up on the day and paying at the door. Just get there early. When it opens, there is a bit of a rush. But by 9.30am, it's calmed down a lot. Just don't go after 11am expecting to walk in. You'll be too late.

It was the largest dome in the world until the mid-19th century and remains the largest unsupported concrete dome in the world. Roman engineers achieved this thanks to a well-designed design and the choice of material: Roman concrete.

We arrived on foot from the Trevi Fountain, we took only 8 minutes walk from each other. Arriving at the pantheon, we thought we would not be in line because we already had the tickets in hand, but we still had to get 10 to 15 minutes in line. Inside the pantheon we fought space with a crowd. The place is very beautiful, but it is for a quick visit.
Every time I come to Rome I can't stop not passing him. The majestic. Too bad it can't be visited freely like it was a couple of years ago.

The cost is 5 euros but it is usually quite crowded and the queue is long. It is worth visiting. The outside is already worth seeing.You have to be careful abroad with the many scams. There are many people seeking signatures through deception. There are Africans who greet you to scam you too. Be careful with all that and with the pickpockets.

Tour was good but don’t go on Sunday mornings as Mass will make it hard to hear guide. Fast track can be achieved by buying a ticket online. Tour line the same as solo online buyers. Small church so buy online ticket and download app for tour information and save your money.
Huge, beautiful, Like everything else many people! And still a mess to get home despite the cuts.
To do with the audio-guide that gives you relevant explanations one about the Roman building that remained as at the time of construction

We thought the Pantheon was amazing. Absolutely spectacular and well worth a trip. We had booked tickets in advance via trip advisor however we felt this was unnecessary. The booking arrived in my email with “click here for tickets” that I had downloaded. However these were not admissible at the site and we had to walk back 5 mins to collect the tickets before we could enter. Tickets were easily available and much cheaper if you pay on arrival instead. We had also paid for an audio guide. The guide was excellent  but again we could have scanned the QR code with our own phones and accessed the same guide this way.
Today, it is still a huge crowd of tourists.Tourist attractions across Italy are overtourist.The Pantheon appears to have been eventing.

An enormously grand church whose outside sits majestically in the surroundings. It's exterior is far better than the inside, which is now charged 5 euro per person. Perhaps better on a rainy day to see the water pour in through the open ceiling but fairly mediocre otherwise. Won't spend much more than 30 minutes. The outside ticket vending machine doesn't need to be used as there is a ticket office inside

Be wary of those who offer to skip the line at ridiculous prices. The ticket costs 5€ and there are two rows on either side of the entrance. Left only cash, right cards. At the center comes those who already have the ticket. Worth the cost. Another thing to keep in mind: the official app promoted inside is paid Visit made during the bridge of May 1, 2025: there was the world 😂
The skip the line feature was worth the cost. The Parthenon was very busy but worth seeing. The tour was a self guided audio tour which I didn’t get much out of. Would recommend an in person guide

They also have coffee grounds or beans to go. METER: Children under 10 years old do not pay. If you buy tickets you have a certain number of hours to use them. If they expire DO NOT THROW THEM AWAY because it is cheaper to "reload" them on the same machine than to buy a new one.  Learn this word: USCITA, it means "exit."  Subway doors won't open if you stick your hand or foot in, be careful. There's a yellow line on the floor, stay back.STRIKES: We stayed 4 days in Rome and we got 2 strikes and a march in front of the hotel. There are many strikes all over Italy. Strikes are announced a day or two in advance and there is always a schedule or some line that does guarantee service, but you have to be aware. Check the news a day before your walk or ask at your hotel.TAXIS: Taxis in Rome can only be taken from certain places and there are huge queues. Mandatory monument to visit if you pass by Rome and you have some time to lose in line the Pantheon for me has never been anything exciting (in fact I have never rushed to go and see it when in the eternal city), but surely it is necessary to make it known to the Family to give a fuller picture of Rome and its history.Outside the line, as in these times, it is really stressful: up to two hours at certain times just to view a circular building with little inside to see (apart from the tomb of Raphael, some Kings and little more).I noticed instead that many tourists, forgetting that this is more like a real church, enter to protect themselves from the weather or the warm weather, and bivouac sitting on the pews for hours thanks to the very low entrance fee.Even in unsuitable clothes (and in fact the guardians inside have a lot to do to dress semi-naked women and little men with panzone in plain sight!).When we went, late afternoon, but with over 30 degrees temperature, for example tourists noticed inside more than interested in any things to see were competing to sit and rest a little '.And maybe nibble and drink the things bought from the stupid stalls and dingy little bars around.That said, however, for those who have not had a visit to the Pantheon must be done, if only to observe the curious roof "with the hole" ("oculus"), a hole that nowadays does not really serve any purpose, but that at the time was believed to allow the gods to "spy" on visitors and astronomers of that time to better scrutinize the sky.Nice to observe when it rains: inside, in fact, the water does not enter even in the presence of heavy rainfall, except in very modest quantities (something that the gratings inside can very well handle, as they have done for centuries and centuries).

When in Rome you have to visit the Pantheon. Earlier last year (Feb. 2023) it was still free, now they charge entry fees...Join a good tour, you will get more out of it, especially if you can avoid long chaotic tourist lines.The Pantheon ( from Greek Πάνθειον Pantheion, "[temple] of all the gods") is a former Roman temple and, since AD 609, a Catholic church (Basilica Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome, Italy. It was built on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa during the reign of Augustus (27 BC – AD 14); then, after the original burnt down, the present building was ordered by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. AD 126. Its date of construction is uncertain, because Hadrian chose not to inscribe the new temple but rather to retain the inscription of Agrippa's older temple.The building is round in plan, except for the portico with large granite Corinthian columns (eight in the first rank and two groups of four behind) under a pediment. A rectangular vestibule links the porch to the rotunda, which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening (oculus) to the sky. Almost two thousand years after it was built, the Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome !The height to the oculus and the diameter of the interior circle are the same, 43 metres (142 ft).It is one of the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, in large part because it has been in continuous use throughout its history. Since the 7th century, it has been a church dedicated to St. Mary and the Martyrs (Latin: Sancta Maria ad Martyres), known as "Santa Maria Rotonda". The square in front of the Pantheon is called Piazza della Rotonda. The Pantheon is a state property, managed by Italy's Ministry of Cultural Heritage and Activities and Tourism through the Polo Museale del Lazio. In 2013, it was visited by over six million people.The Pantheon's large circular domed cella, with a conventional temple portico front, was unique in Roman architecture. Nevertheless, it became a standard exemplar when classical styles were revived, and has been copied many times by later architects

The Pantheon in Rome is one of the most significant buildings in the world that remain from the Roman Empire. Once the Antheon was used as a Roman temple, now it's an important church. The one on the left was for paying with cash.We started waiting in line at 2:05pm ahead of our 3pm guided tour time. Another staff member scanned our entry tickets again and then gave us a paper ticket that a guard then ripped off. Not sure what the point of that was. The system seemed a bit chaotic and they clearly are still ironing out issues. We were directed to the audio guide counter to show our ticket for the guided tour. At this point it was 2:45pm.After all that we were told to wait by the audio guide counter and then our guide met us at exactly 3pm. We were the only ones in the tour so we had a private tour! Our guide was fantastic and clearly very passionate. She also had great historical diagrams and photos on her ipad that were very interesting to me as an engineer.Overall the experience was great and the Pantheon is well worth visiting. However I've taken a star off because of the hectic ticketing system and that we still had to wait 40 minutes in the line even though we'd bought 'reserved' tickets online.
No one to tell us where to go so very confusing. Get in the general admission line then get your audio equipment. Not sure we really needed to pay for a reservation.

The Pantheon is always impressive with its carved ceiling, thinking about the craftsmanship to build such a structure 2000 years ago is just mind blowing. We enjoyed looking around both the inside and the exterior, though the interior was incredibly crowded. There are now metal corrals for tourists to queue in (weren't there when we visited 10 years ago) which help somewhat but also detract from the front steps of the building where you used to be able to stand or sit between the pillars while admiring the giant front doors.
Booking the audio tour in advance made it much quicker on the day. Good info, the narrators were kind of dull.  Amazing building, every nook and cranny.

Friendly staff and easy to understand audio tour. I didn't give it 5 stars because the audio tour didn't follow a path around the building. So we heard info on one area then had to turn around to another area.

Nicely to walk around is the spacious & grand circular floor which is likely "one of marble" (although not to walk through the floor for some reason) but really not much time is needed I would say with just "pretty plain" altar & small alcoves in the wall among which is the tomb of Victor Emmanuel II the "1st king" of Italy (all plain with only statues of saints & such and with nothing else of additional decorations).  And apart from looking around is also to look up to the interior of the "sizeable dome" above with some elaborate designs & such, and actually also pretty impressive to look up not inside but just before entering this temple are these "great pillars" of nice lengths & sizes (with "some time" to view those pillars in my case since there was a line-up to enter starting from the small square outside then through the pillars to get to the temple entrance).Located at just about a 15-minute walk north from the Victor Emmanuel II Monument building (with "2 chariot sculptures" on its top & a number of statues around the building), the visit to this temple was followed by those to: the Loyola Church (with a beautiful "ceiling mural" which can also be viewed in the "slanted mirror" on the floor); the Trevi Fountain (with a set of great statues at a little higher up and water flowing from under them to the pool a little below); and the Spanish Steps (a "pretty wide" staircase between buildings along both sides and with a small church all the way at the top).  And yes, the area overall is also "quite a crowded one" with many narrow streets which are also lined with plenty of souvenir shops (kind of an "old town" area I would say with many colorful heritage buildings but among them also some grand-stately ones with front pillars & such).

This is a former Roman temple and, since 609 AD, a Catholic church (Basilica di Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome. Originally  on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Agrippa during the reign of Augustus, It was rebuilt by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. 126 AD. The building is cylindrical with a portico of large impressive granite Corinthian columns.  It is regarded as probably the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, mainly due to it being kept in continuous use. The Pantheon is free to visit, but you may have to stand in line as they control the amount of people inside at any one time. You can hire an audio guide for about 8.5 euro's or have the full guided tour for about 25 euro's. It is open every day 9.00 am – 7 pm (last entrance at 6.45 pm).

I tried to visit on a Sunday in January but the line was huge.  Returned during the week and no problema getting in right a away.  I had wanted to visit while it was raining but alas the rain stopped when I got there.  It was pretty cool.  Walk around the outside to get the full 'gee this is old' experience.

Panthoen comes from the Greek words, Pan which means ALL and Theon, which means GODS.

Unlike a couple of hours earlier, by midday the area was now packed and we waited for 20 minutes in the queue.  We actually joined a tour along with a Scottish couple to learn a bit about the history.  The guards in the building were obsessive about face masks and mask positioning which was quite ironic as most people in the queue didn't even put them on until they went in!  The guide explained about this being one of the earliest Imperial buildings although it was then repaired and rebuilt by Hadrian at the turn of the first century. Unlike most of the pre Christian temples, this wasn't destroyed by over zealous Christians and was converted into a church.  The design was quite incredible with the dome slowly decreasing in thickness as it climbed and the building materials being swapped for lighter versions, eventually ending at pumice for the very top.  The oculus at the centre is open to the elements and represents the pathway to heaven, being where Romulus was allegedly transported to heaven after being struck by lightning.  The dome is apparently the largest unsupported dome in the world and Michealangelo built the dome of St Peters half a metre smaller as a sign of respect.

The Pantheon is a church in the centre of Rome that was formerly a Roman temple that was thought to have been the private temple of Marcus Agrippa, the son-in-law of Augusta. The building is a chapel in the shape of a rotunda, but with a front entrance built in the form of a Neoclassical Portico. The interior is an enormous rounded room covered by a giant dome, with niches around the room for chapels.

Tip # 1-Forget buying tickets in advance. I tried to get advance tickets online from Canada from the official Pantheon website which was a total disaster. After 4 unsuccessful tries, I gave up.  Tip # 2-Avoid the first Sunday of the month (since most museums are free for Italians and the lines can be very long), instead we showed up on a week day in November and waited 10 minutes in the cash line ( 5 Euros per person) and got in. All the other people who bought tickets in advance waited in longer lines. Basically, don't bother with advance tickets. Once inside you can stay for as long as you like. The Pantheon (an architectural wonder) is totally worth seeing. Tip # 3-Watch Manuel Bravo ( an architect) on You Tube for his in depth explanation of the Pantheon. His videos about various ancient buildings in Italy were very educational and probably provided more information than some guides.

The Pantheon in Rome was built by Emperor Hadrian around 2,000 years ago, around 126 AD. Before it was converted into a Christian church, it served as a temple dedicated to all the pagan gods of ancient Rome. Today, the Pantheon also houses the tombs of several iconic historical figures. Later, it was consecrated as a Christian basilica and dedicated to the Virgin Mary.Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio) (d. 1520) – The world-renowned Renaissance painter and architect. His tomb is marked with the inscription:Here lies Raphael, by whom Nature feared to be outdone while he lived, and when he died, feared she too would die.We had the fast track ticket which we picked up the day before and our time window was between 12- 1pm. We reached there by 1 pm and looked over the temple within 50 minutes. There are no WC/ toilets near the area expect from the few resturants  that provide it for their guests but many of them were unclean.

Don't both with the skip-the-line tickets. If you arrive around 9.30am or 10am, you can walk right in and buy a ticket. Just get there early. When it opens, there is a bit of a rush. But by 9.30am, it's calmed down a lot. Just don't go after 11am expecting to walk in. You'll be too late.

It was the largest dome in the world until the mid-19th century and remains the largest unsupported concrete dome in the world. Roman engineers achieved this thanks to a well-designed design and the choice of material: Roman concrete.

Today we talk about sustainable building, but the Romans could teach us a thing or two about this. They left us the Pantheon, now commissioned as a church, a structure, about 20 centuries old. Despite being neglected sometimes through the centuries, (Bernini scavenged the bronze roof to construct the Baldacchino in the St. Peter) today the Pantheon stands tall as a major monument in a monument-rich  Rome. Inside are the tombs of the Italian kings and the tomb of the famous medieval painter Rafael. Observe also the hole in the immense cupola to let the light in. There was a time when you could walk into the Pantheon unhindered, now you have to pay 5 euros a head for admission. Except on the first Sunday of the month. Then admission is free.

The Pantheon of Agrippa or Pantheon of Rome.Temple commissioned by Marcus Agripap during the rule of Augustus.It was completed by order of Emperor Hadrian and dedicated around 126 AD.The original cover consisted of bronze trusses said to have been despoiled by Pope Urban VII, who had them cast to make a hundred cannons for Castle Sant'Angelo.Quite a few queues are queued, but they're fast.

The place is small, the dome huge, impressive. It is worth putting it on your list and making time to visit. The Pantheon was the inspiration for the Duomo of Florence, who in turn inspired that of St. Peter and the White House in Washington, D.C. No need to buy tickets online (we went in October at 5:30pm and only did 10 min). Outside there are many people in the square, be careful with pickpockets and take the children by the hand if they are boys because in the crowd they can lose sight of you. Inside there is room to sit because it is a church. You need to dress with knees and shoulders covered.If you need to go to the bathroom, buy a coffee. One of the best gelatories we tried in Italy is on the side. The coffee "La Casa del Caffe Tazza D'oro" we liked, they have a great variety, good quality in coffee and has bathrooms. They also have coffee grounds or beans to go. METER: Children under 10 years old do not pay. If you buy tickets you have a certain number of hours to use them. If they expire DO NOT THROW THEM AWAY because it is cheaper to "reload" them on the same machine than to buy a new one.  Learn this word: USCITA, it means "exit."  Subway doors won't open if you stick your hand or foot in, be careful. There's a yellow line on the floor, stay back.STRIKES: We stayed 4 days in Rome and we got 2 strikes and a march in front of the hotel. There are many strikes all over Italy. Strikes are announced a day or two in advance and there is always a schedule or some line that does guarantee service, but you have to be aware. Check the news a day before your walk or ask at your hotel.TAXIS: Taxis in Rome can only be taken from certain places and there are huge queues. You find them on google maps, they are usually close to very tourist places like the Pantheon or the Colosseum. There is no UBER but limousine service, very expensive. Don't trust me to be able to take a taxi.

Come scriveva Stendhal: "The Pantheon is the most perfect thing we have left of Roman architecture." It was originally a temple dedicated to all gods, built by urban planner Agrippa in 27 BC. It became a church thanks to Pope Boniface IV, who received it as a gift in 608 AD from an emperor of Byzantium and took the name of S. Maria ad Martyres. Inside is the tomb of Raphael Sanzio, between the fifth and sixth chapels. Noteworthy is the dome, which has a huge opening in the center, the only source of light. Admission costs € 5 for adults, minors enter for free.

we unplanned went on a day when it was free, the lines look super long but because there are no tickets it moves really quick. There is Santa Maria Sopra minierva around the corner which I preferred a lot more and is free too. I think i would have maybe dropped a star if wasn’t.

Mandatory monument to visit if you pass by Rome and you have some time to lose in line the Pantheon for me has never been anything exciting (in fact I have never rushed to go and see it when in the eternal city), but surely it is necessary to make it known to the Family to give a fuller picture of Rome and its history.Outside the line, as in these times, it is really stressful: up to two hours at certain times just to view a circular building with little inside to see (apart from the tomb of Raphael, some Kings and little more).I noticed instead that many tourists, forgetting that this is more like a real church, enter to protect themselves from the weather or the warm weather, and bivouac sitting on the pews for hours thanks to the very low entrance fee.Even in unsuitable clothes (and in fact the guardians inside have a lot to do to dress semi-naked women and little men with panzone in plain sight!).When we went, late afternoon, but with over 30 degrees temperature, for example tourists noticed inside more than interested in any things to see were competing to sit and rest a little '.And maybe nibble and drink the things bought from the stupid stalls and dingy little bars around.That said, however, for those who have not had a visit to the Pantheon must be done, if only to observe the curious roof "with the hole" ("oculus"), a hole that nowadays does not really serve any purpose, but that at the time was believed to allow the gods to "spy" on visitors and astronomers of that time to better scrutinize the sky.Nice to observe when it rains: inside, in fact, the water does not enter even in the presence of heavy rainfall, except in very modest quantities (something that the gratings inside can very well handle, as they have done for centuries and centuries).

You can buy tickets the day off. There are two lines - one for cash and one for credit card. The cash line seemed to move a lot faster. Lines were manageable in the morning got longer in the afternoon.

When in Rome you have to visit the Pantheon. Earlier last year (Feb. 2023) it was still free, now they charge entry fees...Join a good tour, you will get more out of it, especially if you can avoid long chaotic tourist lines.The Pantheon ( from Greek Πάνθειον Pantheion, "[temple] of all the gods") is a former Roman temple and, since AD 609, a Catholic church (Basilica Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome, Italy. It was built on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa during the reign of Augustus (27 BC – AD 14); then, after the original burnt down, the present building was ordered by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. AD 126. Its date of construction is uncertain, because Hadrian chose not to inscribe the new temple but rather to retain the inscription of Agrippa's older temple.The building is round in plan, except for the portico with large granite Corinthian columns (eight in the first rank and two groups of four behind) under a pediment. A rectangular vestibule links the porch to the rotunda, which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening (oculus) to the sky. Almost two thousand years after it was built, the Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome !The height to the oculus and the diameter of the interior circle are the same, 43 metres (142 ft).It is one of the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, in large part because it has been in continuous use throughout its history. Since the 7th century, it has been a church dedicated to St. Mary and the Martyrs (Latin: Sancta Maria ad Martyres), known as "Santa Maria Rotonda". The square in front of the Pantheon is called Piazza della Rotonda. The Pantheon is a state property, managed by Italy's Ministry of Cultural Heritage and Activities and Tourism through the Polo Museale del Lazio. In 2013, it was visited by over six million people.The Pantheon's large circular domed cella, with a conventional temple portico front, was unique in Roman architecture. Nevertheless, it became a standard exemplar when classical styles were revived, and has been copied many times by later architects

The Pantheon in Rome is one of the most significant buildings in the world that remain from the Roman Empire. Once the Antheon was used as a Roman temple, now it's an important church. We weren’t sure if we would be able to get into the Pantheon, we didn’t have advance tickets and we had heard that the queues were usually long  and you might wait for ages.On arrival we found that the queues were  indeed long and the ticketing system a little confusing, people were offering us tickets and tours but we ignored them as were unsure if they were genuine.There were several queues, some for prepaid tickets and some to buy a ticket….we eventually discovered from the quite helpful ticket staff that we could buy tickets online using the QR code on their display board… this took about a minute and we got in almost immediately.Once inside we  were so glad that we were seeing this amazing building that even today defies modern science as to exactly how it was constructed.It has the original Roman doors….imagine that.It was busy but not overly busy, we could explore and read the information about the church and it’s history, we did not have a guided tour but we will when we return.It’s an amazing place, we gazed up at the dome and tried to imagine all the people who had passed the same way in the previous ages, fantastic and well worth visiting.I recommend you have a guide of some kind and allow plenty of time to absorb just being there as well as the beauty of this ancient building.

We visited the Pantheon today (5th July 2023), which is just the third day that you now have to buy a ticket to enter (previously it was free). Beforehand I bought tickets to the guided tour on the official pantheon website (not the audio guide, an actual tour with a person) and also bought entry tickets online. These were provided to me in two separate ticket PDFs. However even if you buy tickets online, we were still told to wait in the line for the tickets. Please note when facing the pantheon there are two lines. At the time we were there, the line on the right was for buying tickets with a credit card. The one on the left was for paying with cash.We started waiting in line at 2:05pm ahead of our 3pm guided tour time. When we were close to the entry doors a staff member checked our tickets and then escorted us to the front of the line, saving us maybe 10 minutes more waiting.  Another staff member scanned our entry tickets again and then gave us a paper ticket that a guard then ripped off. Not sure what the point of that was. The system seemed a bit chaotic and they clearly are still ironing out issues. We were directed to the audio guide counter to show our ticket for the guided tour. At this point it was 2:45pm.After all that we were told to wait by the audio guide counter and then our guide met us at exactly 3pm. We were the only ones in the tour so we had a private tour! Our guide was fantastic and clearly very passionate. She also had great historical diagrams and photos on her ipad that were very interesting to me as an engineer.Overall the experience was great and the Pantheon is well worth visiting. However I've taken a star off because of the hectic ticketing system and that we still had to wait 40 minutes in the line even though we'd bought 'reserved' tickets online.

Nicely to walk around is the spacious & grand circular floor which is likely "one of marble" (although not to walk through the floor for some reason) but really not much time is needed I would say with just "pretty plain" altar & small alcoves in the wall among which is the tomb of Victor Emmanuel II the "1st king" of Italy (all plain with only statues of saints & such and with nothing else of additional decorations).  And apart from looking around is also to look up to the interior of the "sizeable dome" above with some elaborate designs & such, and actually also pretty impressive to look up not inside but just before entering this temple are these "great pillars" of nice lengths & sizes (with "some time" to view those pillars in my case since there was a line-up to enter starting from the small square outside then through the pillars to get to the temple entrance).Located at just about a 15-minute walk north from the Victor Emmanuel II Monument building (with "2 chariot sculptures" on its top & a number of statues around the building), the visit to this temple was followed by those to: the Loyola Church (with a beautiful "ceiling mural" which can also be viewed in the "slanted mirror" on the floor); the Trevi Fountain (with a set of great statues at a little higher up and water flowing from under them to the pool a little below); and the Spanish Steps (a "pretty wide" staircase between buildings along both sides and with a small church all the way at the top).  And yes, the area overall is also "quite a crowded one" with many narrow streets which are also lined with plenty of souvenir shops (kind of an "old town" area I would say with many colorful heritage buildings but among them also some grand-stately ones with front pillars & such).

This is a former Roman temple and, since 609 AD, a Catholic church (Basilica di Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome. Originally  on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Agrippa during the reign of Augustus, It was rebuilt by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. 126 AD. The building is cylindrical with a portico of large impressive granite Corinthian columns.  It is regarded as probably the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, mainly due to it being kept in continuous use. The Pantheon is free to visit, but you may have to stand in line as they control the amount of people inside at any one time. You can hire an audio guide for about 8.5 euro's or have the full guided tour for about 25 euro's. It is open every day 9.00 am – 7 pm (last entrance at 6.45 pm).

Unlike a couple of hours earlier, by midday the area was now packed and we waited for 20 minutes in the queue. The guide explained about this being one of the earliest Imperial buildings although it was then repaired and rebuilt by Hadrian at the turn of the first century. Unlike most of the pre Christian temples, this wasn't destroyed by over zealous Christians and was converted into a church.  The design was quite incredible with the dome slowly decreasing in thickness as it climbed and the building materials being swapped for lighter versions, eventually ending at pumice for the very top.  The oculus at the centre is open to the elements and represents the pathway to heaven, being where Romulus was allegedly transported to heaven after being struck by lightning.  The dome is apparently the largest unsupported dome in the world and Michealangelo built the dome of St Peters half a metre smaller as a sign of respect.

The Pantheon is a church in the centre of Rome that was formerly a Roman temple that was thought to have been the private temple of Marcus Agrippa, the son-in-law of Augusta. The building is a chapel in the shape of a rotunda, but with a front entrance built in the form of a Neoclassical Portico. The interior is an enormous rounded room covered by a giant dome, with niches around the room for chapels.

The Pantheon was originally built by Marcus Agrippa in 27BC, but was burnt down a few times until Hadrian re-erected the current structure in 120AD. Hadrian kindly left the original dedication to Agrippa above the entrance. Hadrian was less kind to his architect, Apollodorus of Damascus. After a disagreement over the design, Hadrian had Apollodorus executed! But Apollodorus's design has stood the test of time.  For 1300 years, the Pantheon had the largest dome in the world. Today it still has the largest unsupported dome in the world. It's bigger than the dome in the US Capitol (142 ft compared to 96 ft) and the distance from the floor to the top of the dome is exactly twice its diameter. The sixteen massive Corinthian Columns at the front each weighs 60 tons and came all the way from Egypt. Overall, this temple to all the gods, which survived from ancient times only because it was converted into a Christian church in 609AD, remauns one of the most impressive Ancient Roman structures in modern Rome.

Suggest early morning or late afternoon visit - to avoid busiest times. If you choose to visit on your own without a guide, you may want to read up on the history and significance of the dome, oculus, interior, facade detail, inscription, etc. to make the most of your visit.

The Basilica of Santa Maria & the Martyrs, also known as the Pantheon, was formerly a Roman temple, but is now a church which was dedicated as a memorial to all the Christian Martyrs in the 7th century. You enter up stairs and walk between large granite Corinthian columns, but once inside you find that the building is cylindrical.  It reminded me of a huge dome, and is in fact the worlds largest unreinforced concrete dome, sitting on the ground; it is powered by natural light via a central oculus open to the sky. Very different - as there as no seats, it is obviously just a memorial and not used for services.

A hot and humid day for our visit. We arrived about midday. We visited this site on our own, without a tour guide/group. There was a transit strike - apparently somewhat common in Italy - but they do announce them ahead of time. We walked from the hotel to the pantheon - about a mile. There is a plaza (piazza) in front with a fountain. It is a religious building so the girls had to put on their shawls to cover their shoulders and I, of course, took my hat off. However, unlike the Sistine chapel we did not have to have something long enough to cover our knees. I saw this building in an architecture class many years ago and had always wanted to see it in person. It was an incredible site. The ability to build such a large, open space in ancient times is very impressive. The opening in the center is an interesting touch that also contributed to reducing structural load. It would be interesting to see it when it is raining. The first king of Italy is entombed there in one of the alcoves. There are other famous dignitaries there as well. The outside of the building may not seem that impressive with its unadorned stone and its squat dome - but remember how long ago it was built and how impressive the space it creates is. The inside has a number of aspects to appreciate ... altars, statues, paintings, columns... marble, stone... candelabras... etc. it would be great to see an actual mass conducted in the building. It was definitely crowded inside but it was not so bad that you couldn’t move or get up close to the different sections. We did not have to wait to get in. There are also a number of wooden pews if you want to sit down and pray, reflect or simply quietly appreciate your surroundings. It is easy to see why this is one of the most popular attractions in Rome.

Walking through the Place Saint Genevieve past the shrine of the saint Genevieve and church of Saint-Étienne-du-Mont, we approached The Panthéon, deep in the heart of Paris on top of the Montagne Saint-Genevieve, the historic hill of the Latin Quarter.  The Pantheon was originally built as a church to hold the remains of 5th century Saint Genevieve, it is now a burial place for famous French heroes. Designed by Jacques-Germain Soufflot and Jean-Baptiste Rondelet, it combines the look of the Pantheon's portico with 24 large Corinthian columns and the small commemorative tomb, Tempietto (Italian: 'small temple') by Donato Bramante, in Rome.    Construction started in 1758 and finished during the French Revolution in 1790. Its base design was that of a Greek cross with massive portico of Corinthian columns. The dome features three superimposed shells, similar to the St. Paul’s Cathedral in London. Above the entrance in Latin it says "To great men, the grateful homeland” Artist Puvis de Chavannes did many of the interior paintings and mosaics. The crypt covers most of the building underground area, and holds the tombs of some of Frances greats men, like Soufflot, its architect, Voltaire, Jean Moulin, Louis Braille, Victor Hugo, and Émile Zola. In 2002 Alexander Dumas remains were interred here.  There is a 220 feet pendulum beneath the central dome, which is a copy of physicist Léon Foucault original iron sphere, that is now on display in the Musée des Arts et Métiers.  TIP: Go early before the crowds and tour groups.

Astonishing architecture. Demonstrates all the might of Roman Empire. But interior is so-so. Let's say not for the art lovers.
Iconic facade. Less impressive inside than other monuments in Rome. No fee or security so minimal wait times.
Do your research before you go so you know what you are looking at. Eg: God’s eye.  Get there early as line can be big although once it starts moving you move pretty quickly.

Ok, if this was any other city in the world (outside Greece), I would have rated this higher. The reality is that Rome has so many places to see that the Pantheon was just a bit over rated in my book. However, I was happy to have seen it!

We had a brief visit and meant to come back at a less busy time - although we walked around the outside, we didn't make it back inside. I learned later that it's possible to stay for evening Mass, which is now on our list for a future visit.
Had lunch in the area before singing in the church. Visited Pantheon inside, alot of people but not impossible.
This church is so different to others do to its form. Loved it. My only complaint:I would like to know more about the different art and sculptures - there was not a lot to go by once you were inside.
2 years ago I had the opportunity to visit during daylight hours. Although amazing it is nothing like the view in the dark with lights.
Very beautiful bulding, but too many tourists around to be able to really enjoy it. Beware of pickpockets in the area.
Extremely crowded. Pouring rain, so very slippery floor, everyone was carrying an umbrella. Children's choir was performing and it was very beautiful and added to the experience!

Beautiful, stunning architecture. What spoils a lot of Rome’s beautiful buildings is that touts selling tat are permitted to park their carts directly in front of the steps leading to these buildings. It spoils the photo opportunities and I feel detracts from the meaning of some of these historical buildings.
We just walked in off the street and went straight inside. No cue and no charge. Stay as long or as short as you want to.

I love stopping into this place as it is so beautiful and amazing that it was built so long ago. Only reason for four stars is that it is no longer a quiet peaceful place you can pop into like it once was. There is a line out the door and you have to wait until enough people leave to be ushered in. Place is packed!
A bucket list item that was just like walking into an art history textbook. Beautiful space but a bit crowded - we went back at different times in the day and found the crowds varied.

24 years ago, we walked straight into the interior and were able to contemplate in peace the incredible unsupported dome and the many shrines around the walls. Not so this time, the floor was packed, the hubbub was disturbing and the chances of that exclusive shot free of tourist bodies nonexistent. For all that, the dome is a marvel, just don't stand directly under the central opening on a rainy day!

I didn't actually know we could go inside, nor that it is free to do so. Pantheon is an impressive building with all its pillars and artworks inside. As always with popular landmarks like this, please be careful of your belongings (pickpockets).
Another spot in Rome that's a must to visit but again, very touristy outside, but nonetheless worth it.
It was awesome and very large. It’s just the people that make it less appealing since such a crowd doesn’t make the place inviting or comfortable.
You Will see many sights in Rome but not many that are In their original state. Built by the Romans .tip check out YouTube .
After hearing and reading a great deal about the Pantheon it was in real life a little disappointing. A bit like any Great Cathedral in the UK.

When visiting Pantheon in the morning there was a exhibition so it was close until 12:30, we descided to go back later but if you are going with a group it could be worth asking them in advance how they do when this happens.When standing in line it went really fast since everyone goes basicly just around and then out again and there is no money beeing paid. Its important that you take off your hat and are silent in the building, if not you will be told.If you wish to get a good photo of the dome you will get this one just as you enter the building and go outside the building so dont hold the line, keep the line flowing. This building is one of the most preserved in rom and was during the acient time a place to worship the goods and later on became a memorytempel for important people in Rome.
It's definitely worth seeing but I wouldn't stand in line for an hour. Go early and beat the crowd. It's a magnificent piece of architecture.
We actually did not go inside however we sat across the way and had pizza. The area is very busy and is very close to the Trevi fountain; another popular attraction
Great place to visit, unfortunately too crowded. Amazing feet of construction considering its age. You could see it many times and discover new sites.
The rotundra is closed at night and whilst the restaurants are a little expensive the stetting provides a wonderful setting.
Just like most tourist sites in Rome, get there early to beat the crowds.  Plus, be wary of any strangers approaching with "gifts".
It great building but once again you should know about its religious and historical facts otherwise your visit will be just following the tourists !
Can get very busy, and some visitors want to take photos at the most awkward moments.  Important contribution to the understanding of Roman culture, the art work inside is stunning.
Very crowdy the road to arrive at is very beautiful you must walk, but always keeo silent inside ,lots of guards to keep the silence
nice place, very busy, line gets really long in the afternoon. So go in the morning. Its a church now so thats whats inside. But still a cool place to visit
Very beautiful and interesting. But, we were kind of hoping to see a little history of the time when it was a Roman Temple.  It's a church now.

Agrippa was one of the great generals of the Roman Empire. A loyal soldier and right hand of Augustus for whom he built it, though completed at the time of Hadrian. A great Roman architecture and engineering. But its purpose and beauty now corrupted by catholic rituals. Best to see it very early in the morning before the hoard of tourists.
